evidence_entries.evidence_text: The queried woman-in-gray Inner City Tennis drill appears in two intervals: 117.00s-119.00s and 120.00s-128.00s.
The 117.00s-119.00s establishing shot shows text identifying the location as Inner City Tennis.
In the indoor court segment, the female instructor wears a grey t-shirt and black shorts.
Retrieved sampled frames at 120.00s, 121.00s, 122.00s, 123.00s, 124.00s, 125.00s, 126.00s, 127.00s, and 128.00s covering the full 120.00s-128.00s drill window.

In the sampled drill frames, one frame is described as clearly showing the woman tossing a ball up with her right hand, and another frame is described as appearing to show her tossing or about to toss the ball to the child. Other ball-related frames are interpretive or may depict the aftermath of a toss rather than a distinct additional toss.
Among the sampled drill frames, only one frame is described as looking like a side step or recovery movement; the evidence does not ground multiple distinct side-step events across the full window.

inference_steps.text: The relevant scene remains the Inner City Tennis volunteering/drill segment, anchored by the establishing shot naming Inner City Tennis and the indoor-court sequence with the woman in gray in the 120.00s-128.00s window.
The woman in gray is the female instructor on the indoor court, wearing a grey t-shirt and black shorts.
The new frame retrieval covers the full drill window from 120.00s through 128.00s, so the counting evidence now spans the relevant sequence rather than only an early portion of it.

Within those sampled frames, the woman is visually counted for two ball tosses: one frame appears to show her tossing or about to toss the ball to the child, and another clearly shows her tossing a ball up with her right hand. Other ball-in-air frames are better treated as aftermath or continuation, not separate additional tosses.
Within the same sampled frames, only one frame is described as a side step or recovery movement, so the grounded side-step count is one.
Using the grounded counts from the sampled full-window frames, the ratio of side steps to ball tosses is 1 to 2.
A 1:2 ratio does not match any of the provided answer choices, so no listed option can be justified from the grounded evidence.

findings.message: The trace does not answer the multiple-choice question with one provided option. It concludes that the grounded ratio is 1:2 and that no listed option matches, but the task requires selecting a justified option from the given choices.

The counted side-step and ball-toss totals are only weakly grounded from sampled frames and interpretive descriptions. The evidence itself says one toss only 'appears' to be a toss or pre-toss, and the side-step evidence says one frame looks like a side step or recovery movement, which does not firmly establish distinct event counts for the full drill.

audit_report.feedback: The trace is orderly, but it remains unresolved on an MCQ and relies on tentative frame-level interpretations for the event counts. It needs answer-critical grounding that establishes the distinct counts strongly enough to select one listed ratio.

The scene cuts to a close-up of a tennis ball bouncing on the court, then to a player in a white t-shirt with 'CHAMPLIN PARK REBELS' printed on it, smiling as she plays.

transcript_segments.text: Alex Replier calls the tennis court her happy place. The Chamberlain Park Junior tried a lot of sports growing up, but tennis stuck. It was just something about the fact that when I'm on the court, it's me and I get to make decisions. And if you know I messed up, it's me. And if I win, it's me.

Replier started playing when she was five years old and made the CP varsity as a seventh grader. In ninth grade, she became the Rebels' number one singles player. I went from just being the flattest, hardest hitter ever to someone who I can actually hit topspin now and just really, I know how to construct points better and I know how to really just set up a strategy that'll work to have me be successful on the court.

Replier is a team captain this season and tries to be a leader and mentor to younger players. She really took me under her wing. She just made sure I felt like I could talk to her about anything. And if I had any questions, I could go to her. Year after year, she got a little more mature. She started to make friends. And she's really blossomed into the leader of Temple Park tennis.

transcript_segments.text: Replier is always striving to get better and to that end she plays on the club circuit as well. I love competing in tournaments whether they're local level or national level and the club environment is just so fun and it is something I really love to do is just compete and every weekend you know we're traveling to different cities and spending a lot of time on the court.

Still, Replier says the club tennis world is very different from high school and can sometimes be difficult to manage. I've kind of built a community with my family and with my coaches where, you know, I have a supportive group around me, but definitely when you're on the court, you're just kind of sitting on the bench yourself. It definitely can get a little, feel a little lonely. When she's not playing tennis or keeping up with her schoolwork, Alex Replier is probably teaching the game. She volunteers at Inner City Tennis in Minneapolis.

captions.visual: A female instructor in a grey t-shirt and black shorts stands on an indoor tennis court, smiling as she teaches a young girl in a pink shirt. The instructor tosses a multi-colored ball in the air and prepares to hit it with her tennis racket. The words 'INNER CITY TENNIS' are visible on the wall behind them.

transcript_segments.text: It's the greatest feeling. Just seeing these kids kind of latch on to a sport that I love so much and just the smiles on their face when they kind of, you know, hit a forehand for the first time or, you know, something just clicks in their head where they're like, oh my god, I know how to do this. It's just a really amazing feeling to know that I have an impact on their lives and that I get to show them something that I love so much and see them love it just as much.

Champlin Park bowed out early in the section tournament, but with a pretty young squad, Replier believes the future is bright for Rebels tennis. All of these girls work so hard in the offseason, and we all play constantly almost every day in the offseason. And so as we look into the future, we know that even if this year isn't perfect, we have time to grow, and we can do even better next year and just keep working hard. Jason Melillo, CCX Sports.
Find more prep sports games and highlights at ccxmedia.org and follow us on social media.
